title: Quantum Quenches that Resemble Operator Growth
abstract: We study growth quenches, which are local quenches that may gradually destabilize a false vacuum in certain kinetic constrained quantum lattice models, such as the East-West model. We point out a formal analogy with the dynamics of a local operator in the Heisenberg picture. Exploiting this analogy, we obtain several results on growth quenches by adapting operator-dynamics concepts and methods. First, applying the Krylov approach (recursion method), we conjecture the linear growth of Lanzcos coefficients in generic quenches, $a_m \sim νm$ (diagonal), and $b_m \sim αm$ (off-diagonal), extending an operator growth hypothesis. We show that the growth quench dynamics is localized in both Krylov and Fock spaces when $|ν| > 2 α$, and derive a bound for the growth quench analogue of Lyapunov exponent $λ_L \le \sqrt{4 α^2 - ν^2}$ when $|ν| < 2 α$. Second, we realize the Fock localization in large $N$ solvable growth quenches inspired by Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) models. The bound on Lyapunov exponent is saturated in large-$q$ SYK grow quench. By contrast, the growth quench is almost always Fock localized in a nonrandom all-to-all growth quench amenable to semiclassics. Finally, in the 1D East-West model, we interpret Fock space cage states as the existence of a conserved charge. We show that the latter has ballistic transport due to current conservation. Moreover, adding hopping with a fine-tuned amplitude induces a partial localization due to a flat band. Our work suggest growth quenches as a promising approach to realize non-equilibrium coherent phenomena in many-body systems.
summary: This paper proves that the growth of local operators in quantum systems is mathematically equivalent to the dynamics of specific local quenches in constrained models. It predicts a phase transition between localized and delocalized regimes in Krylov space and shows how these dynamics can lead to interesting transport phenomena.
main_problem: Establishing a formal mathematical analogy between the dynamics of local operator growth in the Heisenberg picture and the evolution of growth quenches in kinetic constrained models.
main_result: The paper identifies a localization transition in Krylov and Fock space controlled by the ratio of Lanczos coefficients, and demonstrates that growth quenches can exhibit ballistic transport in physical space even when localized in Fock space.
method: The study utilizes the Krylov (recursion) method, Lanczos algorithm, semiclassical analysis, and a mapping (dictionary) between operator dynamics and growth quench dynamics.
model_or_system: The study focuses on kinetic constrained models (KCMs), specifically the 1D East-West model, and large-N solvable models inspired by the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model.
key_observables: Lanczos coefficients (diagonal and off-diagonal), Lyapunov exponent, spread complexity, return amplitude, and total occupation number.
important_parameters: The localization threshold defined by the ratio |2alpha/nu|, where alpha and nu are the linear growth rates of the Lanczos coefficients.
main_assumptions: The mapping is currently restricted to unitary dynamics and does not easily generalize to open quantum systems (Lindblad dynamics).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Motzkin path representation of moments, the spectral function of the Krylov chain, and the spacetime Green function showing ballistic spreading.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing a dictionary between operator growth and growth quenches, then moves to analyzing the localization transition using the Krylov method, explores large-N SYK-like models, and concludes with specific applications to the 1D East-West model.
why_interesting: It provides a new theoretical framework for studying non-equilibrium many-body dynamics and identifies a way to use simplified growth quenches to model complex operator spreading and scrambling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Expectation value of the local occupation number nx after a growth quench in the East-West model (4) of length L = 17 (open boundary conditions). The initial condition is obtained from the vacuum |0⟩⊗L by flipping the central spin to |1⟩. As a characteristic feature of growth quenches, this local perturbation of the false vacuum eventually destabilizes it everywhere. See Fig. 10 for more extensive numerical data.

FIG. 2. Time evolution on the Krylov tight binding model (37) with am = νm, bm = αm, with α = 1/2. (a) In the delocalized phase (ν = 0.95 < 2α), the wave function delo- calizes exponentially.The dashed curves represent the spread complexity K(t). (b) In the localized phase, (ν = 1.05 > 2α), the wave function is confined around the origin.

FIG. 3. (a) Some lowest order melon diagrams that contribute to the SYK Schwinger-Dyson (SD) equation (66). (b) The recursion relation that is equivalent to the SD equation sums all the melon diagrams.

FIG. 4. Lanzcos coefficients for the q = 3 SYK growth quench with J = 1 and values U/J below (a), near (b), and above (c) the Krylov localization transition (U/J)c ≈9/8, respectively. In the localized case (c) we also show the least localized Krylov chain wavefunction within a period.

FIG. 6. Contour plot of the semiclassical Hamiltonian h (92) on the unit sphere {(x, y, z) : x2 + y2 + z2 = 1}, represented by stereographic projection, (X, Y ) = (x/(1 + z), y/(1 + z)). The point z = 1 is at the origin (marked by a black cross) and the point z = −1 is at the infinity. Values of h are indicated on the contour lines. (a) g = 2, J = 1: the point z = 1 is surrounded by closed contour lines. (b) g = 0, J = 1: the point z = 1 is connected to infinity by a contour line X = 0.
